title: How to handle the grief that surfaces when life gets good
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The grief that comes when things finally get better

metaDescription:
The grief that comes when things finally get better 

 I remember sitting in my therapist's office three years into what I thought was "healing." Things w...
htmlContent:
The grief that comes when things finally get better

I remember sitting in my therapist's office three years into what I thought was "healing." Things were good. I mean actually good. My job was stable. My relationships had stopped feeling like minefields. I wasn't waking up in a cold sweat every night. And I was miserable.

Not the kind of miserable that comes from a bad day or a breakup. The kind that sits in your bones and whispers "you don't deserve this." The kind that makes you scan the horizon for the disaster that must be coming. Because that's what your nervous system learned to do. Stay with me here.

When your life has been shaped by complex trauma, your brain and body develop a very specific relationship with safety. Safety feels unfamiliar. It feels suspicious. And when it finally arrives, grief often arrives right alongside it. This isn't a sign that you're doing something wrong. It's a sign that your system is trying to make sense of something it never had before.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body as incomplete survival responses. When life gets good, those incomplete responses start to stir. They want to be completed. They want to be felt. And that feeling can look a lot like grief.

Here's the thing. Your nervous system doesn't know how to rest in safety if safety was never modeled for it. It's like asking someone who has only ever breathed shallowly to suddenly take a deep, full breath. The body resists. It doesn't trust the air.

What this grief actually is

Let me name what I think is happening here, because nobody told me and I spent years thinking I was broken in some new and special way.

When life gets good, you're not just experiencing the present moment. You're also experiencing the absence of all the moments that should have been good but weren't. You're grieving the childhood you didn't have. The relationships that hurt you. The years you spent in survival mode. The version of yourself that never got to just be a kid.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades showing us that trauma is not just an event in the past. It's a current reality in the body. So when you finally feel safe enough to let your guard down, all that stored survival energy starts moving. It has to go somewhere. And often, it comes out as tears. Or anger. Or a sense of sadness that doesn't seem to have a clear cause.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how recovery happens in stages, and that grief is not a sign of failure. It's a sign that you're actually processing. That you're letting the past be the past instead of carrying it forward like a weight you forgot you were holding.

I want you to notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your chest tighten? Did your throat get tight? Did you feel a wave of something you can't quite name? That's the grief. It's not here to destroy you. It's here to be witnessed.

Why your nervous system fights the good stuff

Your nervous system has a job. Its job is to keep you alive. Not happy. Not comfortable. Alive. And it learned, through years of experience, that safety was not reliable. That good things were followed by bad things. That letting your guard down meant getting hurt.

So when life gets good, your system doesn't celebrate. It sounds the alarm. "Danger," it says. "This feels like that time before everything fell apart." And you feel the urge to push the good away. To sabotage. To find a reason to be unhappy. Because unhappiness is familiar. Unhappiness is safe.

This is where the grief gets complicated. You're not just grieving what you lost. You're grieving the fact that you can't fully enjoy what you have. That your own brain is working against you. That peace feels like a trap.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. He talks about emotional flashbacks and how they can be triggered not just by bad things, but by good things too. A moment of intimacy. A compliment. A quiet evening. All of these can send your system into a tailspin because they don't match the internal map of what life is supposed to feel like.

Think about it. Your map says life is hard. Life is dangerous. You have to earn safety. You have to be on guard. And then life shows up and says "here's some peace, here's some joy, here's some rest." And your map says "this can't be right. Something is wrong." The grief is the space between the map and the reality. It's the loss of the map itself.

How to actually handle this grief

I'm not going to give you a five-step plan that promises to make it all go away. That would be lying. And you've been lied to enough by the wellness industry that promises transformation in three easy payments of $49.99.

Here's what I know from my own experience and from what I've learned studying people like Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory. The grief needs to be felt in the body, not just understood in the mind. You can't think your way out of this one. You have to let it move through you.

When the grief shows up, here's what I do. I stop. I put my hand on my chest or my belly. I take a slow breath. And I say to myself "this is grief. It's old. It's here because I'm safe enough to feel it now." That last part is key. Your system needs to know that the grief is not a sign of danger. It's a sign that danger is over.

You might need to cry. You might need to scream into a pillow. You might need to take a walk and let the tears come while the wind hits your face. Do it. Don't try to fix it. Don't try to understand it. Just let it be what it is. The grief is not your enemy. It's your system finally letting go of something it's been holding for years.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Boundaries are part of this process because grief often shows up when we start saying no to things that drain us and yes to things that fill us. That shift is disorienting. It's also necessary.

I also want to say something that might be hard to hear. Some of this grief will never fully go away. You will always carry some sadness for the childhood you didn't have. For the love that was withheld. For the safety that was stolen. But over time, that grief becomes less of a weight and more of a quiet companion. It stops running the show. It just sits in the corner, and sometimes you look at it and nod. "I see you. I remember. I'm okay now."

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. He developed Internal Family Systems, and his work helps you see that the part of you that grieves is not the enemy. It's a protector. It's a part that needs to be heard, not silenced. When you can sit with that part and say "I know you're scared. I know you're sad. I'm here with you," something shifts. The grief softens.

When the grief feels too big

There are days when the grief is not a quiet companion. It's a tidal wave. It crashes over you and you can't breathe. You wonder if you're regressing. If all the work you've done was for nothing. If you'll ever feel truly okay.

I've been there. I'll probably be there again. And here's what I've learned. The tidal wave is not a sign of failure. It's a sign that you're in a deep layer of healing. The body can only process so much at a time. When it's ready for the next layer, it will bring it up. And it will feel overwhelming. That's the nature of deep work.

What helps me in those moments is grounding. Feeling my feet on the floor. Noticing something I can see, something I can hear, something I can touch. Letting my breath be slow and full. And reminding myself that this wave will pass. It always does. The grief is not permanent. It's a visitor.

If you're in one of those waves right now, I see you. I'm not going to tell you to look on the bright side or to count your blessings. That's toxic positivity and it has no place here. What I will tell you is that this wave is part of the process. It's not a detour. It's the road.

You are not broken. You are not doing it wrong. You are a person whose nervous system learned to survive in a world that wasn't safe, and now it's learning to live in a world that is. That takes time. That takes grief. And that takes a kind of courage that most people will never understand.

But I understand. And you understand. And that's something.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman - A foundational text on trauma recovery
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker - Deeply resonates with C-PTSD survivors
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab - A practical guide to boundaries
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz - A gentle introduction to parts work
